 args = parser.parse_args()
 
 catalog = open( args.Geometry_cat , "r" )
 Read Input ###################################################.
 
 lines = catalog.readlines()
 
 N_cubes = int( lines[0].split()[3] )
 
 Cube_side_lenght = np.array( lines[4].split()[3:] ).astype( float )
 
 IPD = float( lines[6].split()[3] )
 
 rstart = float( lines[1].split()[3] )
 
 rstop = float( lines[1].split()[4] )
 
 Plc_vertex = np.array( lines[2].split()[3:] ).astype( float )
 
 Plc_direction = np.array( lines[3].split()[3:] ).astype( float )
 
 Aperture_angle = float( lines[5].split()[3] )
 
 replication_origin = np.asarray( replication_origin )
 
 rmin = np.asarray( rmin )
 
 rmax = np.asarray( rmax )
 
 fate = np.asarray( fate )
 
 fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 9), dpi=800)
 Make Plot ###################################################.
 
 ax = fig.add_subplot(221, projection='3d')

Function Documentation

◆ plot_cone()

PlcGeometryplot_3D.plot_cone (   starting_vertex,
  cone_direction,
  cone_aperture,
  ax,
  rstart = 0.0,
  rstop = 1.0,
  N_r = 5.0,
  N_phi = 10.0 
)

Definition at line 76 of file PlcGeometryplot_3D.py.

76def plot_cone( starting_vertex , cone_direction , cone_aperture , ax, rstart = 0.0 , rstop = 1.0 , N_r = 5.0 , N_phi = 10.0 ):
77
78 # Step between radius of concentric circumferences
79
80 deltar = ( rstop - rstart) / N_r
81
82 # Normalizing the cone axis of symmetry
83
84 norm_direction = cone_direction/np.linalg.norm( cone_direction )
85
86 # Find the perpendicular direction to the axis of symmetry
87
88 x_versor = np.array([ 1. , 0. , 0. ])
89 p = np.cross( norm_direction , x_versor ) # Cross product of norm_direction and x_versor in R^3 is a vector perpendicular to both norm_direction and x_versor
90
91 # Check perpendicularity
92
93 if np.linalg.norm(p) == 0: # i.e. p vector is parallel to norm_direction
94
95 z_versor = np.array([ 0. , 0. , 1.])
96 p = np.cross( norm_direction , z_versor )
97
98 # Normalizing p
99
100 p /= np.linalg.norm(p)
101
102 # Find the perpendicular direction to p
103
104 q = np.cross( norm_direction , p )
105
106 # p and q form a basis for the plane perpendicular to the cone axis
107
108 # Plot lateral surface of the cone
109
110 AA = min( cone_aperture , np.pi ) # The aperture angle must be less than or equal to pi. If the aperture angle is less than pi, the cone is truncated at a certain height
111
112 if cone_aperture < np.pi :
113
114 # Loop over polar angle
115
116 for i_phi in range( N_phi ): # N_phi is the number of polar angles at which the generatrixes of the cone will be sampled
117
118 phi_1 = i_phi * 2.0 * np.pi / (N_phi) # Start value
119 phi_2 = ( i_phi + 1 ) * 2.0 * np.pi / (N_phi) # Increased value
120
121 # Loop over the radius
122
123 for i_r in range( N_r ): # N_r is the number of radii
124
125 r_1 = i_r * deltar + rstart # Start value
126 r_2= ( i_r + 1 ) * deltar + rstart # Increased value
127
128 # For each combination of angles and radii, it calculates the position of two points on the surface of the cone using trigonometry and the basis vectors calculated earlier
129 # It then plots a line segment connecting these two points on the given matplotlib axis object. The result is a series of line segments that together form the lateral
130 # surface of the cone. The aperture angle is taken into account when determining the positions of the points, and the number of samples along the surface of the cone is
131 # determined by the input parameters N_r and N_phi
132
133 X = np.array([[ starting_vertex[0] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[0] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[0] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[0] * np.sin(phi_1) ),
134 starting_vertex[0] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[0] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[0] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[0] * np.sin(phi_1) )],
135
136 [ starting_vertex[0] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[0] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[0] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[0] * np.sin(phi_2) ),
137 starting_vertex[0] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[0] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[0] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[0] * np.sin(phi_2) )]])
138
139 Y = np.array([[ starting_vertex[1] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[1] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[1] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[1] * np.sin(phi_1) ),
140 starting_vertex[1] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[1] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[1] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[1] * np.sin(phi_1) )],
141
142 [ starting_vertex[1] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[1] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[1] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[1] * np.sin(phi_2) ),
143 starting_vertex[1] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[1] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[1] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[1] * np.sin(phi_2) )]])
144
145 Z = np.array([[ starting_vertex[2] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[2] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[2] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[2] * np.sin(phi_1) ),
146 starting_vertex[2] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[2] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[2] * np.cos(phi_1) + q[2] * np.sin(phi_1) )],
147
148 [ starting_vertex[2] + r_1 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[2] + r_1 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[2] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[2] * np.sin(phi_2) ),
149 starting_vertex[2] + r_2 * np.cos(AA) * norm_direction[2] + r_2 * np.sin(AA) * ( p[2] * np.cos(phi_2) + q[2] * np.sin(phi_2) )]])
150
151 # Plot of each line segment of the lateral surface
152
153 ax.plot_wireframe(X,Y,Z, rcount=1, ccount=1, color='yellow' , alpha=0.3)
154
155 # Plot the dome of the cone as a 3D wireframe surface with axis along the vector norm_direction, centered at the point starting_vertex
156 # The wireframe is generated by sampling points on the surface using a polar coordinate system and then converting them to Cartesian coordinates
157
158 N_cap = max( int( 1.5 * N_phi * AA / np.pi) + 1, 5 ) # Determines the number of points along the circular cross-section of the wireframe that is being plotted
159
160 # Specify the step sizes of the meshgrid in a single variable
161
162 a = complex( 0 , N_phi+1 ) # real part 0 and imaginary part N_phi+1, which specifies the step size along the phi dimension
163 b = complex( 0 , N_cap ) # real part 0 and imaginary part N_cap, which specifies the step size along the r dimension
164
165 # Creates a dense grid of points in the polar coordinate system
166
167 u , v = np.mgrid[ 0 : 2*np.pi : a, 0 : AA : b ] # u and v represent the polar coordinates of the sampled points. Meshgrid_shape = ( N_phi+1 , Ncap )
168 # u is the angle [0:2*np.pi]
169 # v is the radius (in rad)
170
171 # Cartesian coordinates
172
173 x = starting_vertex[0] + rstop * ( np.cos(v) * norm_direction[0] + np.sin(v) * ( p[0] * np.cos(u) + q[0] * np.sin(u) ) )
174 y = starting_vertex[1] + rstop * ( np.cos(v) * norm_direction[1] + np.sin(v) * ( p[1] * np.cos(u) + q[1] * np.sin(u) ) )
175 z = starting_vertex[2] + rstop * ( np.cos(v) * norm_direction[2] + np.sin(v) * ( p[2] * np.cos(u) + q[2] * np.sin(u) ) )
176
177 # Plot the dome surface
178
179 ax.plot_wireframe(x,y,z, rstride=5, cstride=5, color='yellow', alpha=0.5)
180
181
182# Input #
183
min
#define min(a, b)
Definition collapse_times.c:1350
max
#define max(a, b)
Definition collapse_times.c:1351

References max, and min.

◆ plot_cube()

PlcGeometryplot_3D.plot_cube (   shifted_origin,
  side_lenght,
  fate,
  ax 
)

Functions definition ##########################################.

Definition at line 12 of file PlcGeometryplot_3D.py.

12def plot_cube( shifted_origin , side_lenght , fate , ax ):
13
14 # Dictionary with a different colors for a different fate of the cube face
15
16 if ( fate > 0 ):
17
18 color={ 0: "gray", # Perché stiamo usando anche zero se c'è un if che dice > 0 ?
19 1: "blue", # Starting cube
20 2: "cyan", #
21 3: "palegreen", #
22 4: "turquoise" } #
23
24 # Control Transparency
25
26 alpha={ 0: 0.1,
27 1: 0.3,
28 2: 0.3,
29 3: 0.3,
30 4: 0.3 }
31
32 # Generating vertices of a unitary cube
33
34 base_vector = [ 0.0 , 1.0 ]
35 vertices = np.array(list(product( base_vector, base_vector , base_vector ))) # Cartesian product of the base vector
36
37 # Translation of generic vertices on the vertices of box replications that are used to sample the light cone
38
39 for i in range(3):
40
41 vertices[ : , i ] *= side_lenght[i] # L is the side length of the cube
42 vertices[ : , i ] += shifted_origin[i] # C is the starting vertex of the cube (starting and replications)
43
44 # Scatter plot of all vertices generated before
45
46 ax.scatter3D( vertices[: , 0], vertices[: , 1] , vertices[: , 2] , s=5 )
47
48 # Creating X , Y , Z base data point for plotting the cubes using plot_surface
49 # X and Y are a 2D array of points x and y while Z is used to indicate the 2D array of heights for x and y points
50
51 X , Y = np.meshgrid( base_vector , base_vector )
52 Z = np.ones(( 2 , 2))
53
54 # Plotting the faces of the cubes
55 # We have to plot each face separately because it could potentially have a different color from the others according to the fate value
56
57 ax.plot_surface(X*side_lenght[0] + shifted_origin[0] , Y*side_lenght[1] + shifted_origin[1] , Z*shifted_origin[2] , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Bottom Z face
58 ax.plot_surface(X*side_lenght[0] + shifted_origin[0] , Y*side_lenght[1] + shifted_origin[1] , Z*(side_lenght[2] + shifted_origin[2]) , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Top Z face
59 ax.plot_surface(X*side_lenght[0] + shifted_origin[0] , shifted_origin[1] , Y*side_lenght[2] + shifted_origin[2] , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Right Y face
60 ax.plot_surface(X*side_lenght[0] + shifted_origin[0] , side_lenght[1] + shifted_origin[1] , Y*side_lenght[2] + shifted_origin[2] , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Left Y face
61 ax.plot_surface( shifted_origin[0] , X*side_lenght[1] + shifted_origin[1] , Y*side_lenght[2] + shifted_origin[2] , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Left X face
62 ax.plot_surface( side_lenght[0] + shifted_origin[0] , X*side_lenght[1] + shifted_origin[1] , Y*side_lenght[2] + shifted_origin[2] , alpha = alpha[fate], color = color[fate]) # Right X face
63
64# Plot distance
65

◆ plot_dist()

PlcGeometryplot_3D.plot_dist (   C,
  L,
  V,
  r,
  ax 
)

Definition at line 66 of file PlcGeometryplot_3D.py.

66def plot_dist( C , L , V , r , ax ):
67
68 vers = ( C + L/2 - V ) /np.linalg.norm( C + L/2 - V )
69
70 ax.plot( np.array([ V[0] , V[0] + vers[0] * r ]),
71 np.array([ V[1] , V[1] + vers[1] * r ]),
72 np.array([ V[2] , V[2] + vers[2] * r ]))
73
74# Plot the cone lateral surface and the dome
75
